does: SafeAPT introduces a simulation-to-real learning algorithm that addresses safety during policy transfer by maintaining a diverse repertoire of policies evolved in simulation and selecting the safest candidate for real-world deployment. The method iteratively learns a probabilistic reward model and a safety model by combining real-world observations with simulated priors, then performs Bayesian optimization over the policy repertoire to identify high-performing policies that satisfy predefined safety constraints. Through episodic real-world interaction, SafeAPT adaptively refines both models and safety guarantees, avoiding damage to the robot or environment during the critical adaptation phase when reality gap effects are most pronounced.
connects: This work builds on the established simulation-to-real transfer learning paradigm in robotics, which has long grappled with the reality gap problem as policies optimized in simulation often fail unsafely in the real world. SafeAPT combines insights from quality-diversity optimization and evolutionary algorithms, which maintain diverse solution sets rather than converging to single optima, with Bayesian optimization and probabilistic modeling approaches used in sample-efficient real-world adaptation. The paper extends prior work on constrained reinforcement learning and safe robot learning by incorporating simulated policy priors directly into the selection and adaptation process, moving beyond naive policy transfer toward actively maintained safety models during real-world learning.
enables: SafeAPT points toward more scalable and practical robot learning systems that can autonomously adapt to real environments while certifying safety properties beforehand. The approach enables robots to operate in safety-critical domains where damage during learning is unacceptable, expanding the applicability of simulation-to-real methods beyond controlled settings. Future work could leverage the diverse policy repertoire for transfer across different robot embodiments or tasks, extend the safety model learning to handle complex constraint types, or integrate formal verification methods with the learned safety models to provide stronger guarantees. This framework also suggests pathways for bootstrapping safer exploration policies from diverse simulated behaviors rather than random initialization.
